source.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

claim_chain: Antarctic temperature change follows a persistent spatial pattern across timescales
Temperature variability increases with mean surface temperature at each site
This pattern is inconsistent with simple Planck response
A temperature-dependent greenhouse effect feedback explains the pattern
The feedback arises from nonlinearity at cold Antarctic temperatures
Deviations from the pattern indicate regional forcing such as ice-sheet elevation changes
West Antarctic elevation history can be reconstructed from deviations

typical_pattern: Provide mechanistic explanation or physical basis
Address exceptions or deviations from pattern
Connect to broader implications or physical understanding

likely_priorities: Physical mechanism must be clearly distinguished from alternative explanations
Timescale independence of pattern must be robustly demonstrated
Quantitative agreement between observations and theory
Consistency with independent geological and modeling evidence
likely_attacks: Ice-sheet elevation changes could explain the pattern instead of greenhouse feedback
Spatial inhomogeneity in forcing may dominate at short timescales
Lags expected from slow feedbacks are not observed
Pattern may not hold when forcing variability exceeds mean forcing
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly addresses ice-sheet elevation as an alternative explanation, stating 'It is thus unlikely that the pattern of Antarctic temperature response identified here can be attributed primarily to changes in ice-sheet elevation.' It acknowledges that 'not all of the spatial variability in Antarctic temperature change can be attributed solely to the GHE feedback' and discusses conditions under which 'The imprint of the GHE feedback will be less pronounced if the gain due to the feedback is less than the ratio of the spatial variability in the forcing to the mean forcing.' The paper defends against slow-feedback explanations by noting 'If the observed temperature-change pattern were to arise from a slow feedback, such as changes in ocean circulation...lags would be observed compared to the mean change across different timescales.' These self-defenses reveal the authors' anticipation of reviewer concerns about alternative mechanisms, timescale dependence, and the dominance of the proposed feedback.

examples.quoted_phrase: Our results demonstrate that there is a fundamental pattern of temperature change in Antarctica
the nonlinearity of the GHE feedback accounts for the overwhelming majority of the differences among Antarctic ice-core temperature records
This is expected if a fast-acting feedback process, directly related to surface temperature, is locally amplifying any forced changes
may lead to regional variation in forcing
It is thus unlikely that the pattern of Antarctic temperature response identified here can be attributed primarily to changes in ice-sheet elevation

phrase_evidence.phrase: Our results show that, across a range of timescales, the pattern of Antarctic temperature change is a function of the absolute surface temperature
This is expected if a fast-acting feedback process, directly related to surface temperature, is locally amplifying any forced changes
Of course, not all of the spatial variability in Antarctic temperature change can be attributed solely to the GHE feedback
These observations can be understood by considering the feedback factor
Over intervals of large mean change in Antarctic temperature and over longer timescales, the mean forcing must be large compared to inhomogeneity in the forcing
It is thus unlikely that the pattern of Antarctic temperature response identified here can be attributed primarily to changes in ice-sheet elevation
we identify unshared patterns of temperature variability at all sites
The results are robust to reasonable choices in the surface lapse rate
This reconstructed surface elevation change is consistent, in both timing and magnitude, with geological evidence
Our results demonstrate that there is a fundamental pattern of temperature change in Antarctica
The simplicity and timescale independence are critical features of the observed pattern
They indicate that the response is independent of the nature of the initial forcing
Deviations from the pattern arising from the GHE feedback are expected if the pattern of forcing is not uniform
Importantly, the implied elevation change is smaller than some previous estimates suggest
Antarctica is our planet's Southern radiator
The efficiency of its response to an energetic forcing depends on the surface temperature
We identify a persistent pattern of change in which the temperature variability of an Antarctic site increases with its mean surface temperature
This pattern is inconsistent with the Planck response
a temperature-dependent feedback explains the fundamental pattern of temperature change
The feedback arises from a nonlinearity of the greenhouse effect, evident only at the cold surface temperatures of the Antarctic
This feedback may be initiated by any mean energetic forcing and thus manifests across all timescales
Local deviations from the expected pattern of temperature change indicate regional forcing
finding a history that is supported by geological and glaciological evidence and consistent with ice-sheet modeling
Inset shows locations of cores on the Antarctic continent
Circles show the temperature change for each ice-core site
colored by mean Holocene temperature
with best-fit lines, colored by the mean temperature change

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, author names, or invented numbers appear in the output. The profile is based entirely on the provided discussion, conclusion, abstract, and figure legend sections.

_phrase_violations.phrase: Antarctica is our planet's Southern radiator
